title: Using the MMU and Program Creation: Compilation, Linking, Relocation, Shared Libraries
subtopics: Intel MMU: segmentation and pagination in practice

definitions.description: Translating a source file into an object file containing machine instructions; each module starts at address 0.
Combining multiple object files into a single executable; resolves external references and performs relocation.
Adjusting absolute addresses in object code when modules are placed at their final memory positions.
Header, entry point table, external reference table, code, and relocation dictionary.
A library loaded into memory once and shared by multiple programs; reduces disk and memory usage.
The OS automatically loads required shared libraries when the program starts, based on import libraries statically linked into the executable.
The program explicitly loads/unloads shared libraries at runtime using system calls (LoadLibrary/dlopen).
Code that uses only relative addresses (relative to PC); can be loaded at any memory address without relocation.

algorithms.name: Linker operation
steps: Build a table of all object modules and their sizes
Assign start addresses to each module (cumulative sizes)
Add relocation constant (= module start address) to each address in the code
Resolve external references: insert addresses of functions/data defined in other modules

examples.name: Relocation solutions comparison
examples.description: 4 approaches: relocation info in executable (DOS), relocation register, position-independent code, memory pagination.

learning_objectives: Describe how Intel hardware implements segmentation and pagination in practice
Explain how Windows and Linux use the MMU
Evaluate the trade-offs of using the MMU
Describe the stages of program creation (compilation and linking)
Explain the structure of an object file
Explain the relocation problem and four solutions
Compare implicit and explicit dynamic linking
